text: Welcome to a video documenting robotic thoracic surgery here at James Cook University Hospital.
In this case, we are observing a procedure called a robotic right-sided excision of a bronchogenic cyst and a pericardial patch repair to the right bronchus intermedius.
This also happens to be the first robotic patch repair of the right main bronchus to be reported in the UK.
And as we can observe, Mr. Joel Dunning has just excised the visceral pleura and has done some general draining as well of the blood and other fluids in the cavity.
And as he starts to progress with his excision through the pleura, you can start to observe a large
rather large bronchogenic cyst starting to appear in sight.
This cyst was found in the subcarinal region, compressing the main bronchus
and posterior end of the heart in a 45-year-old female patient.
You can also observe the esophagus is easily seen and there does not seem to be any connections with the mucosa of the esophagus.
Hence, therefore, we believe that it is less likely to be an esophageal cyst or even a pericardial cyst.
And once again, there is more excision.
And over here, you can see Mr Dunning actually excising a piece of lymph node that has taken out to be handed to the assistant.
And again, more excision of the pleura.
And as Mr. Dunning continues performing the surgery, you can observe some amount of cystic fluid beginning to appear as white creamy material as he perforates it.
He then proceeds to drain and remove the cystic fluid from the bronchogenic cyst.
that all the cystic fluid is taken out from the cystic pouch.
After aspirating most of it, Mr. Dunning then takes a closer inspection of the bronchogenic cyst cavity to ensure that all the cystic fluid has been cleared.
Mr. Dunning then begins to excise the cyst and then slowly proceeds to the other parts of the cyst as well.
he carefully separates the cyst from the membranous portion by gently dissecting it.

However, despite that, unfortunately, the cyst was very tightly associated with the bronchus intermedius, and upon separating it from the membranous portion of the bronchus, the membranous portion came apart, as it was a very thin layer.
Therefore, it was in our suspicion that this cyst
was likely to have arisen from the bronchus symptomedius, which had also been the cause of the patient's bad cough.
Eventually, he succeeds in removing the entire cyst.
Now that Mr Dunning has completely dissected and removed a cyst, he assesses the bronchus for any defects or abnormalities.
And here we can observe him tying the bronchial artery with some sutures to prevent further bleeding.
He then proceeds to remove and clear some blood clots within the bronchus.
After that, he puts in a pericardial patch, which is to be prepared to use in order to seal the
torn membranous portion of the bronchogenic cyst.
Mr Dunning has to carefully excise the pericardial patch to suitable dimensions that would then be suitable to repair the membranous portion.
As he begins his first suture in order to attach the pericardial patch to the area
And quite interestingly, Mr Dunning uses a 2.4cm long pericardial patch in order to seal the perforation.
In terms of suture materials, Mr Dunning had used two 4-0 V-lock sutures in order to stitch the pericardial patch to the bronchus.
It is interesting to note, however,
That one key advantage with V-lock sutures is that surgeons do not need to tie a knot at the start of the suturing, but rather they can just make a loop which makes the suturing all the more easier.
And as Mr Dunning finishes his final knot on the pericardial patch, he then cuts off and removes the excess suture material.
He then begins an underwater test in order to see if the pericardial patch was indeed working.
And no bubbles have appeared so far.
He then begins to check if there are any other points of leakage.

and begins to secure the suture with two old ticron sutures in order to ensure that the stitching on the pericardial patch is strong and tight to prevent any possible leakage in the future and of course you see he tightens his last few knots
pericardial patch and again more underwater tests in order to see if the suturing was tight and of course ensuring a firm and secured pericardial patch is essential
Finally more underwater tests to be observed if there is any air leakage.
And of course to top it off with some Evisil glue.
And he finishes his operation with a final underwater test.
Thank you for watching.
